analogy: Think of "Nationalism in India" through the chapter's own opening images: 29 Nationalism in India As you have seen, modern nationalism in Europe came to be associated with the formation of nation-states. It also meant a change in people’s understanding of who they were, and what defined their identity and sense of belonging. New symbols and... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 1 The First World War, Khilafat and Non-Cooperation, 1.1 The Idea of Satyagraha, 1.2 The Rowlatt Act, 1.3 Why Non-cooperation?. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Nationalism in India is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that 29 Nationalism in India As you have seen, modern nationalism in Europe came to be associated with the formation of nation-states.

The text explains that it also meant a change in people’s understanding of who they were, and what defined their identity and sense of belonging.

The text explains that new symbols and icons, new songs and ideas forged new links and redefined the boundaries of communities.

The text explains that in most countries the making of this new national identity was a long process.

The text explains that in India and as in many other colonies, the growth of modern nationalism is intimately connected to the anti-colonial movement.

The text explains that people began discovering their unity in the process of their struggle with colonialism.

The text explains that the sense of being oppressed under colonialism provided a shared bond that tied many different groups together.

The text explains that but each class and group felt the effects of colonialism differently, their experiences were varied, and their notions of freedom were not always the same.

The text explains that the Congress under Mahatma Gandhi tried to forge these groups together within one movement.

The text explains that but the unity did not emerge without conflict.

The text explains that in an earlier textbook you have read about the growth of nationalism in India up to the first decade of the twentieth century.

The text explains that in this chapter we will pick up the story from the 1920s and study the Non- Cooperation and Civil Disobedience Movements.

The text explains that we will explore how the Congress sought to develop the national movement, how different social groups participated in the movement, and how nationalism captured the imagination of people.

The text explains that nationalism in India Chapter II Nationalism in India Fig.

The text explains that mass processions on the streets became a common feature during the national movement.

The text explains that india and the Contemporary World 30 1 The First World War, Khilafat and Non-Cooperation In the years after 1919, we see the national movement spreading to new areas, incorporating new social groups, and developing new modes of struggle.

The text explains that as you know, he had come from South Africa where he had successfully fought New words Forced recruitment – A process by which the colonial state forced people to join the army Fig.

The text explains that when the marchers were stopped and Gandhiji arrested, thousands of more workers joined the satyagraha against racist laws that denied rights to non-whites.

The text explains that in 1917 he travelled to Champaran in Bihar to inspire the peasants to struggle against the oppressive plantation system.

The text explains that it gave the government enormous powers to repress political activities, and allowed detention of political prisoners without trial for two years.

The text explains that on 10 April, the police in Amritsar fired upon a peaceful procession, provoking widespread attacks on banks, post offices and railway stations.

The text explains that they have made the religion of non-violence their own ...’ Source Source A Read the text carefully.

The text explains that some came to protest against the government’s new repressive measures.

The text explains that there were strikes, clashes with the police and attacks on government buildings.

The text explains that the government responded with brutal repression, seeking to humiliate and terrorise people: satyagrahis were forced to rub their noses on the ground, crawl on the streets, and do salaam (salute) to all sahibs; people were flogged and villages (around Gujranwala in Punjab, now in Pakistan) were bombed.

The text explains that it should begin with the surrender of titles that the government awarded, and a boycott of civil services, army, police, courts and legislative councils, schools, and foreign goods.

The text explains that then, in case the government used repression, a full civil disobedience campaign would be launched.

The text explains that thousands of students left government-controlled schools and colleges, headmasters and teachers resigned, and lawyers gave up their legal practices.

The text explains that but this movement in the cities gradually slowed down for a variety of reasons.

The text explains that so students and teachers began trickling back to government schools and lawyers joined back work in government courts.

The text explains that you are a student in a government-controlled school.

The text explains that as tenants they had no security of tenure, being regularly evicted so that they could acquire no right over the leased land.

The text explains that source Source B In 1928, Vallabhbhai Patel led the peasant movement in Bardoli, a taluka in Gujarat, against enhancement of land revenue.

The text explains that here, as in other forest regions, the colonial government had closed large forest areas, preventing people from entering the forests to graze their cattle, or to collect fuelwood and fruits.

The text explains that not only were their livelihoods affected but they felt that their traditional rights were being denied.

The text explains that when the government began forcing them to contribute begar for road building, the hill people revolted.

The text explains that for plantation workers in Assam, freedom meant the right to move freely in and out of the confined space in which they were enclosed, and it meant retaining a link with the village from which they had come.

The text explains that can you think of a similar example from the national movement in Indo-China (Chapter 2)?

The text explains that within the Congress, some leaders were by now tired of mass struggles and wanted to participate in elections to the provincial councils that had been set up by the Government of India Act of 1919.

The text explains that against this background the new Tory government in Britain constituted a Statutory Commission under Sir John Simon.

The text explains that set up in response to the nationalist movement, the commission was to look into the functioning of the constitutional system in India and suggest changes.

The text explains that in an effort to win them over, the viceroy, Lord Irwin, announced in October 1929, a vague offer of ‘dominion status’ for India in an unspecified future, and a Round Table Conference to discuss a future constitution.

The text explains that apart from Mahatma Gandhi, you can see Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel (extreme left), Jawaharlal Nehru (extreme right) and Subhas Chandra Bose (fifth from right).

The text explains that the liberals and moderates, who were proposing a constitutional system within the framework of British dominion, gradually lost their influence.

The text explains that the idea was to make the demands wide-ranging, so that all classes within Indian society could identify with them and everyone could be brought together in a united campaign.

The text explains that the tax on salt and the government monopoly over its production, Mahatma Gandhi declared, revealed the most oppressive face of British rule.

The text explains that people were now asked not only to refuse cooperation The Independence Day Pledge, 26 January 1930 ‘We believe that it is the inalienable right of the Indian people, as of any other people, to have freedom and to enjoy the fruits of their toil and have the necessities of life, so that they may have full opportunities of growth.

The text explains that then choose any three and write about their hopes and struggles to show why they joined the movement.

The text explains that discuss the Salt March to make clear why it was an effective symbol of resistance against colonialism.

The text explains that imagine you are a woman participating in the Civil Disobedience Movement.

The text explains that explain what the experience meant to your life.

The text explains that why did political leaders differ sharply over the question of separate electorates?

Source: NCERT Class 10 India and the Contemporary World II, Chapter 2: Nationalism in India

revision_bullets: 29 Nationalism in India As you have seen, modern nationalism in Europe came to be associated with the formation of nation-states. (NCERT Class 10 India and the Contemporary World II Ch2 pg 1)
It also meant a change in people’s understanding of who they were, and what defined their identity and sense of belonging. (NCERT Class 10 India and the Contemporary World II Ch2 pg 1)
New symbols and icons, new songs and ideas forged new links and redefined the boundaries of communities. (NCERT Class 10 India and the Contemporary World II Ch2 pg 1)
In most countries the making of this new national identity was a long process. (NCERT Class 10 India and the Contemporary World II Ch2 pg 1)
In India and as in many other colonies, the growth of modern nationalism is intimately connected to the anti-colonial movement. (NCERT Class 10 India and the Contemporary World II Ch2 pg 1)
People began discovering their unity in the process of their struggle with colonialism. (NCERT Class 10 India and the Contemporary World II Ch2 pg 1)
The sense of being oppressed under colonialism provided a shared bond that tied many different groups together. (NCERT Class 10 India and the Contemporary World II Ch2 pg 1)

But each class and group felt the effects of colonialism differently, their experiences were varied, and their notions of freedom were not always the same. (NCERT Class 10 India and the Contemporary World II Ch2 pg 1)
The Congress under Mahatma Gandhi tried to forge these groups together within one movement. (NCERT Class 10 India and the Contemporary World II Ch2 pg 1)
In an earlier textbook you have read about the growth of nationalism in India up to the first decade of the twentieth century. (NCERT Class 10 India and the Contemporary World II Ch2 pg 1)

prelims_traps: Do not treat 1 The First World War, Khilafat and Non-Cooperation and 1.1 The Idea of Satyagraha as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Nationalism in India; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Nationalism in India through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 1 The First World War, Khilafat and Non-Cooperation and 1.1 The Idea of Satyagraha to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
